Evergreen Memorial Gardens 
Find a Grave memorial # 114131075

facts.value:
Effie Leona (White) Sullivan, 82, of Vancouver, WA, passed away May 13, 2015. She was born in Carson, WA.
Effie loved her family, especially her grandchildren whom she helped raise by running a unique daycare service that provided her direct access to spoiling them.
She enjoyed a lifetime of service at Safe Harbor Church of the Nazarene.
During her decline with dementia, Effie relished going to 'school' at CDM's dementia daycare where she made friends and preferred craft time and holiday parties.
A memorial service will be held on Sat., May 30, 2015, 11:00 a.m., at Safe Harbor Church of the Nazarene, 8100 E. Mill Plain Blvd., Vancouver, WA, 98664.
Donations in Effie's name can be made to CDM Caregiving Services (her beloved dementia daycare) at 2409 Broadway, Vancouver, WA, 98663 or online at www.cdmltc.org.

facts.value:
From personal interview with Larry Leo Sullivan:

I had 5 brother and 2 sisters.  My dad was born in 1895 and my  mom in 1907.  My oldest sister and I the only ones left (ed note:  in Apr 2014).  Ronnie was killed in a car/bicycle accident in 1963.  Jerry, Delos, Bobby and Joyce all died of various causes  between 1995 and 1999, with Orville dying in 2004 (ed note:  actually, 2006).


Yes, the Sullivan's went to Idaho for a few years, but as I understand it, my mom was horribly homesick and were  back in Minnesota by January 1937, when Jerry was born.  Then at the onset of ww2, my dad went to Alaska to work in construction.  Well, there was an homeless girl about 12 when mom and dad were married and she lived with them for a time.  Her name was Carol, and she had married and moved to B Z Corners.  When my dad came back from Alaska he stopped and visited with them.  When he got home,he was all excited to move out west.  My oldest sister,Mildred and her husband,Herb decided to go first in the fall of1945.  My mom and dad came out in June of 1946.   By that time, my sister's husband was very homesick and they went back for a while, thus I have two  nieces, two years apart,one born in Minnesota and one in Washington. That takes us to about 1946 and living in Washington.

William MAGBY/MCBEE b SC abt 1781 was the father of John Alexander b 1813 AL; Samuel b 1823 AL; Silas b 1828 AL;William b 1818 AL;James b 1834 AL; April b date unknown & another daughter, b date unknown.
From 1850 census Pike Co, Arkansas

1830  Census gives Williams age between 40-50. 1840 Census gives Williams age as between 40 to under 50 years. (40-49) That would give birth dates 1791  through 1800. First known child is John Alexander born in 1813, placing William at 22 years old when Alexander was born. If 1830 census is correct, or both are close then he would probably be born about 1790. Just attached 1830 census. He is living next door to what is assumed to be his brother Robert. The families are in Arkansas by 1850, and William can not be found (but those assumed to be his children from the 1830 census are also in Arkansas)

Possibly Walker County, but that did not exist at the time. Many of the family members on the 1820 state census for Alabama (William Magby, the Southerlands, McClendons, McClungs, Sullivans, Beardons, and others who married Vardry's children.) Robert is by the Beardons. Not sure of the family connection, but later in life, after Robert moved to Arkansas, he married Betsy Beardon, who appears to be from this same family. Possible the daughter, or the widow of one of the sons, of James, the man listed in the 1820 Alabama State Census living next door to Robert Magby in 1820.

facts.value:
Joshua Burriss married Sarah Chamblee in N.C. and was born circa 1755 according to Ethel Updike's book, The Chamblee, Shirley, Speers Families of the South. If one of their sons was born in 1774 as has been claimed, then likely Joshua was born before 1753 at the latest and she had to be born by 1758 or thereabouts.

She was said to be related to the Chamblees of Bertie County, Wake County and Hertford County, N.C.

Ed Hillhouse Jr. wrote the following: "Later in life, Joshua and Sarah Burriss moved to Mount Tabor to be near their son James. Joshua died there in 1804 and was buried in the cemetery there beside his wife, Sarah. (NOTE: Joshua Burriss/Boroughs predeceased his wife.) 

This cemetery was moved to the First Baptist Church cemetery when the Saluda Association office was built. Their graves are not marked in any way."

NOTE: The old (original) Mt. Tabor graveyard is/was located at 1901 S. Murray Ave., Anderson, S.C., but then called Orr Mills, now the site of the Saluda Baptist Association.

Mt. Tabor Church moved to 5901 Highway 187, Anderson, and the NEW Mt. Tabor cemetery located there.

First Baptist Church, founded by Rev. James Burriss, moved to 307 S Manning Street, Anderson, and his grave marker was moved there when several of the other markers from the original Old Mt. Tabor Church (Orr Mills) were moved there.

Some later articles and family trees call her Sarah Mary Chamblee but appear to be confusing her with her daughter, Mary (Polly) Burriss Chamblee, said to have married her cousin. No contemporary records refer to Joshua Burriss' wife by another name but Sarah.

facts.value:
"In his father's 1761 lease on 47 acres of Kilraughts (Reg. of Deeds Dublin Book 247 Deed 158814) it states that John was aged 14.  It is written in the 'History of Westmoreland County' that John Borland first sailed to America in 1769 and ended crossing the ocean five times.  He was delayed in coming back to America during the Revolution.  His brother Samuel was supposed to have sailed with him on one of these trips.  The earliest one can find John settling down in America is in 1785 when he was living in Bedford county, Pa.  By 1790 he moved to Westmoreland county, PA where he owned a farm on the NE corner of present day Penn township near a town of Export which he bought from William Ellison, Jr. for 10 shillings an acre.
In 1792bJohn's name appears in the tax list for Franklin township, Westmoreland county, PA.
On 13 Jul 1793 John Borland with his brother James witnessed the will of John Smiley, their nephew, in Westmoreland county, PA. (Source: Will Book #1, pg.110)
 On John's farm was erected the first schoolhouse in the area in 1799.  In 1811 John was taxed on 400 patent acres, 2 horses, 3 cows.  He is said to have died in 1830 shortly before his sister did who is believed to have been married to Judge Potts of Johnstown.  His grave has not been located."   Source of this biography has been lost.

attribution.changeMessage:
(Chilton Times, April 21, 1888)

At the residence of her son, N. Marshall Hephner of Charlestown, Wis., on the morning of the 13th inst., Mrs. Anna Maria Hephner, aged 80 years, 2 months and 22 days. 
The deceased was born in Berwick, Adams County, Penn., and, with her parents at an early age, emigrated to Ohio, and in 1849, again emigrated with her husband, who departed this life twenty-five years ago, to Outagamie County, Wis., removing to this county twelve years ago, residing here since, except a short time which was spent in Kansas, with a daughter. 
She was a Christian lady and leaves the record of a well spent life. She was a faithful, devoted wife, mother and friend, whose acts of hospitality and kindness made lasting endearments. Full of years, loved and venerated by all who knew her, "the rest that remaineth for the people of God," is hers forevermore. The matin bells of a new and never-ending day, chime her welcome to the Eternal city for "there is not night there." Her life radiant with religion and faith, and ever wise counsel, is crowned with immortality and bliss ineffible and undying. 
The funeral obsequies occurred on Monday at St. Augustine church here, and the remains taken to Appleton and was met at the depot by a large number of friends, many of whom were pioneers with the deceased, and then conveyed to Center and interred by the side of her husband in the cemetery selected and donated by them to the St. Edward congregation of that place, and there they rest in the shadow of their pioneer home. Five of thirteen children survive her, all of whom were present at the funeral except Mrs. Mathew Nugent, who resides in Minnesota. 

(Thanks to Rose for the obituary)

facts.value:
It is proved that this Lemuel Drew is not the son of Jedediah Drew and Abigail Esther Whitehouse.
He is likely the son of Samuel Drew and Love Drew
SAMUEL DREW, born by 1750, appears in eastern Newfield in the 1800 census. He was over 45 with a boy aged 10-16, a female over 45 and another aged 16-26. The 1810 census shows the elderly couple a female (still given 16-26) in the household. "Old Mrs. Drew," wife of Samuel, died in Newfield, 4 Jan. 1815 (VR). Note the similarity of this date and the following: Lovey Drew, mother of Robert Drew, d. in Newfield, 2 Jan. 1813 (VR). These are probably two different women but could possibly be the same person with one date recorded incorrevtly.
It is probable that this Samuel was in Barnstead, N. H., in 1790, listed with one male of 16 and over, three males under 16 yrs., and five females in the household.
Numerous correspondents from several states have been interested in the ancestry of Samuel Drew and several other Drews who lived in eastern Newfiled. Various theories have been proposed.  Possibly Newfield's Samuel Drew was bp. 8 Aug. 1742, a son of Thomas (4) (Thomas (3), Francis (2), William (1)) Drew, thus a cousin of Elijah Drew who settled in western Newfield, and a possible brother of Benjamin Drew who went to Shapleigh (see Hy. of Durham, N.H., pp. 144-145).
A second possibility is that Clement (3) (Thomas (2), William (1)) and Mary (Bunker) Drew were grandparents of Newfield's Samuel Drew (see HY. of Durham, p. 143).
A third possibility set forth in the Vineland Historical Magazine (date?), shared by Mildred Pierce, may have been based on family tradition passed down in the family of Clement Drew of Newfield and Eaton. This states that Samuel Drew, b. in Durham in 1722, married Polly Edgerly and had a son Samuel, b. 1756 in Newfield. It also states that this son married Love Bunker who lived to be 106 years of age. This account may have some degree of  accuracy though it contains obvious errors such as placing Samuel's birth in Newfield nineteen years before the town's settlement. And the only known marriage of a Samuel Drew to Polly Edgerly took place in 1795.
Whatever their ancestry, I suspect that Samuel Drew who came to Newfield, probably from Barnstead, was a brother of Robert Drew in the neighboring town of Loudon, N.H., and that the Drews living in eastern Newfield were children of these two men or related to them. (Among those who have been generous in sharing information about these "puzzling" Drews are Mildred Pierce, Judy Webb, Keith Henney, Beatrice T. Bates, Elaine D. Adjutant, Earle A Drew, Robert L. Taylor, Rebecca J. Drew, and William G. Drew)
Information above comes from The Early Families of Newfield Maine by Ruth Bridges Ayers.

facts.value:
William Diffee, Jr. is the son of William Diffee, Sr. and his wife Mary 'Elizabeth' YORK, sister to Jeremiah York I.

The Jeremiah York I family went through a major transition when Sarah Wilson died in 1752. It is most probable that the William Diffee families as close relatives came to visit at Terrapin Neck at this time of grief. Most likely William Diffee, Jr. became well acquainted with Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK during this time period. It is very likely that William Diffee, Jr. age twenty married Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK age twenty six at Terrapin Neck in 1752.

These may have been the events that led to the sale of the 323 acre “Terrapin Neck” property on the beautiful high bluffs of the Potomac River in 1753. Jeremiah sold their farm of 323 acres, her older brothers had begun to marry, move out and establish their own homesteads. Most likely Betty Ann and her new husband William Diffee, Jr. continued to serve the remaining Jeremiah York family with cooking, house keeping and their domestic needs during this time of adjustment.

In less than three years William Diffee, Jr. and his new wife Betty Ann York were joining the wagon train in 1755 migrating with most of the Jeremiah York I families traveling to central colonial North Carolina. The two William Diffee families no doubt had meet and made friends the Julian families who were perhaps part of the same wagon train migrating to North Carolina.

It is observed that Betty Ann and William only started their family after they arrived in 1755 because of their many domestic duties in Virginia. The two Diffee families settled on land in Colonial Orange (now Randolph) County that was near the land grants of their new Julian family friends.

Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK is the fourth child of ten children born to Jeremiah York I and his wife Sarah Wilson in 1726 at West Nottingham, Chester County, Pennsylvania. By 1733 she was living at their new log family cabin on Terrapin Neck, Colonial Frederick County, Virginia (now Jefferson County, West Virginia). No doubt as the only girl among a lot of brothers Betty Ann York and her mother Sarah became great friends and cooks for the Jeremiah York family. Betty Ann had not married because she helped her mother as a most dependable domestic helper for the household work for the large family of men.

The Jeremiah York I family went through a major transition when her mother Sarah Wilson died in 1752. Jeremiah sold their farm of 323 acres, her older brothers had begun to marry, move out and establish their own homesteads. It is most probable that the William Diffee as close family relatives came to visit at Terrapin Neck at this time of grief. Most likely William Diffee, Jr. became well acquainted with Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK during this time period. It is very likely that William Diffee, Jr. age twenty married Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK age twenty six at Terrapin Neck in 1752.

These may have been the events that led to the sale of the 323 acre “Terrapin Neck” property on the beautiful high bluffs of the Potomac River in 1753. Her older brothers were marrying, moving out and establishing their own homesteads. Most likely Betty Ann and her new husband William Diffee, Jr. continued to serve the remaining Jeremiah York family with cooking, house keeping and their domestic needs during this time of adjustment. 

In less than three years William Diffee, Jr. and his new wife Betty Ann York were joining the wagon train in 1755 migrating with most of the Jeremiah York I families traveling to central colonial North Carolina. The two William Diffee families no doubt had meet and made friends the Julian families who were perhaps part of the same wagon train migrating to North Carolina.

It is observed that Betty Ann and William only started their family after they arrived in 1755 because of their many domestic duties in Virginia. The two Diffee families settled on land in Colonial Orange (now Randolph) County that was near the land grants of their new Julian family friends.

 	John Sheffield born 1727 in PA? died about 1796 in Moore Co. He was a Revolutionary War Veteran, and served from Moore Co.

John married about 1748 to Hannah whose surname is thought to be Melton or Smith. Hannah was born about 1730 and died after 1799 (see land grant below) in Moore Co, NC. and considered to be the daughter of Robert and Johanna Smith of Hanover County, VA. or Robert Melton of VA.

 		The will of John Sheffield was dated December 6, 1794, and proven February 1796 in Moore County, NC. The will was witnessed by 		Avert Smith, John Sheffield and Isam Sheffield. Named as executors were Hannah and Avert Smith. Avert Smith was one who ordered 		the will recorded.

John Sheffield’s Will (dated 6 Dec 1794/proven February 1796)

In the name of God Amen. The sixth day of December one thousand seven hundred and ninety four I John Sheffield of Moore County & State of North Carolina being by decay of nature unwell but of perfect mind and memory, thanks be to God, for the same therefore calling to mind the mortality of my body and knowing that it is appointed for all men to die and after death to judgement, I do hereby leave bequeath and dispose of my Goods and chattels in manner and form following that is to say I commit my body to the earth to be buried in a decent Christian manner at the discretion of my executors and likewise my soul into the hand of God, that gave it, knowing that after death I shall come to judgement and as to my worldly affairs I leave bequeath and give in manner and form following that is to say principally and first of all, I leave and bequeath to my well beloved wife HANNAH SHEFFIELD, the plantation whereon I now do live with the buildings, orchards and improvements thereon with one hundred acres of Land, during her widowhood with all my stock & cattle (mpremise?).

 		I leave and bequeath to my son MARK SHEFFIELD one shilling sterling.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my son JOHN SHEFFIELD one shilling sterling.

	I leave and bequeath to my son ADAM SHEFFIELD one hundred acres of land lying on the Rattle Snake branch.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my son ISHAM SHEFFIELD, the land and plantation whereon he now lives containing one hundred 	acres.

 	Item I leave and bequeath to my son AVERIT SHEFFIELD the land and plantation whereon I do now live to enjoy after his mothers 	deceased containing one hundred acres.

	Item I leave to my daughter SARAH DENNIS one shilling sterling.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my daughter MARY DUNN one shilling sterling.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my daughter LUCRETIA DUNN one shilling sterling.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my daughter REBEKAH AWTERY one shilling sterling.

	Item I leave and bequeath to my daughter ELIZABETH AWTERY one shilling sterling and my two youngest daughters LYDDA and 	MILLY SHEFFIELD I leave and bequeath one shilling sterling to each and as to my goods and chattels stock etc. I leave for support 	of my beloved wife HANNAH SHEFFIELD during her life then to dispose of the same acknowledging this and no other to be my last 	will and testament.

 			In witness whereof I have hereunto, set my hand and affixed my seal the and year above written.

		Signed, Sealed and delivered in the presence of us Who in the presence of each other saw the same JOHN SHEFFIELD SR. 		assigned the same

 (signed)                                             (signed)

Avert Smith (his mark)              JOHN SHEFFIELD (his mark)

hereby appoint AVERT (Sheffield - but with SHEFFIELD crossed out) SMITH and my wife HANNAH SHEFFIELD Executors of this my last will and testament. Windsor Pierce (his signature)  (looks like) Moore County February 1796

The Jeremiah York I family went through a major transition when Sarah Wilson died in 1752. It is most probable that the William Diffee families as close relatives came to visit at Terrapin Neck at this time of grief. Most likely William Diffee, Jr. became well acquainted with Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK during this time period. It is very likely that William Diffee, Jr. age twenty married Elizabeth "Betty" Ann YORK age twenty six at Terrapin Neck in 1752.

These may have been the events that led to the sale of the 323 acre “Terrapin Neck” property on the beautiful high bluffs of the Potomac River in 1753. Jeremiah sold their farm of 323 acres, her older brothers had begun to marry, move out and establish their own homesteads. Most likely Betty Ann and her new husband William Diffee, Jr. continued to serve the remaining Jeremiah York family with cooking, house keeping and their domestic needs during this time of adjustment.

facts.value:
Jonathan Wheelock was a Minuteman in the Revolutionary War. He later became a Cavalry Soldier.

https://www.findagrave.com/memorial/107538746/jonathan-wheelock

On 17 June 1776 Petersboro, New Hampshire, United States, Jonathan Wheelock signed "Oath of Fidelity" agreement to serve in Revolutionary War Army on this day.

Jonathan Wheelock served his country during the Revolutionary war, as a private in the New Hampshire troops and was a signer of the Association Test and also a member of the Committee of Safety. In signing the "Oath of Fidelity", he understood that it would be a most terrible death if the fortunes of war turned against the United American Colonies. Here is the test: "We, the Subscribers, do hereby solemnly engage and promise that we will to the upmost of our power, at the risque of our lives, and fortunes, with arms, oppose the hostile proceedings of the British Fleets, and Armies, against the United Colonies". (Signed) Jonathan Wheelock of Petersboro, N.H., June 17, 1776, (Ref.) Page III - Vol. XXX, N.H. State Papers. Batchellor.

facts.value:
John, the 3rd child of Ezekiel and Mary Rust Herrick, was born about 1730, in Coventry, Tolland, Connecticut. There are several sources and family trees that have a different birth date or birth order for John, but in the book Herrick Genealogy by Jedediah Herrick, he is listed as the 3rd son born about 1730. John’s father Ezekiel migrated to Coventry from Massachusetts as a young man in search of fertile available farmland. His parents were married in Coventry and they spent the rest of their lives raising children and farming in Coventry until their death in 1760. 
Many of the records for the Herrick family in Coventry are missing and this is one researcher Susan Whitney Dimock of South Coventry’s explanation in June 14, 1897: “There is a large gap in the records of Coventry, CT. It is especially severe for the Second or North Society region spanning the period 1770-1802. The records of the first church, prior to the year 1766, have either been lost or destroyed and no records of the Second (North Parish) Church seem to have been kept until about 1800.” When John was of age, he courted and married Mary prior to the birth of their children and they were likely married in Coventry from 1757 to 1759. There are no town or church records documenting their marriage or her birth information. Mary’s parents are unknown and it’s also not known if her Bates Hilliard name is a maiden or former married name. 
Mary and John had eleven children listed in the Herrick Genealogy book: the 1st child, a son John in 1760; the 2nd child, son Daniel in 1762; the 3rd child, James about 1764; the 4th child, Ebenezer about 1766; the 5th child, Ezra in 1770; the 6th child, a son Ezekiel about 1771; the 7th child, a daughter Huldah about 1772; the 8th child, a daughter Esther about 1774; the 9th child, Ann about 1776; the 10th child, a daughter Jemima about 1778; and the 11th child, a daughter Sara about 1779. There are no birth or marriage records for many of these children. 
 Several historical records indicate that the Herrick’s 1st, 2nd and 3rd sons didn’t move to the Ohio River Valley, but migrated to Berkshire, Massachusetts: John in 1780, at age 20; Daniel in 1787, at age 25; and Ebenezer in 1789, at age 23. Regrettably, there are no marriage or death records found for the other children. Coincidentally, two of John’s brothers also moved to Berkshire, Massachusetts: his older brother Ezekiel around 1753, at age 25; and his younger brother Zebulon around 1760, at age 27. It’s possible that the migration of both his sons and brothers are connected, but since there are no death records for John and Mary, it’s no known if they left Coventry as well.
American Revolutionary War-SAR Application No. 5773  28 January 1893 by Grove Herrick Wilson Descendant

facts.value:
James Covey b. 1715, son of James b.1687, married Mary Lanphere , They had eleven children.
--
Birth: 24 JAN 1714/15 Westerly, Washington Co., RI
--
Vital Record of Rhode Island, 1636-1850 , first series..
Westerly marriages, publ. 1894
Covey (Corey), James and Mary Lanphere married by Elder John Mason on Jan. 21, 1738-39
--
Connecticut Soldiers, French and Indian Wars,1755-1762, page 249
Covey, James
location.. Connecticut
regiment..First, Lyman, Phineas.. Colonel & Captain company Eighth
Co. commander.... Butler, Zebulon Captain

The data base of Rhode Island Colonial War Servicemen, 1750-1762, listed
James Covey as serving in Captian Brown's Company in 1759 during the French and Indian War. He served from 1756-1763 and fought in many battles, particularly in the Great Swamp campaign of 1761. For his service he was entitled to Bounty land in New York. James' early lot assignment was in the Phillipe Division lot #49 of Dutchess County, New York. The date of the chart is May 2, 1781. This would account for the family moving to New York.
--
The names of James Covey and Mary Lamphere were provided to me by Rita and the Covey Cousin's newsletter on line Prodigy, PVJS64A on 1/30/1996.
  --
There is a two-volume Covey Genealogy published in 1970 starting with James Couve born 1625 ?, arrived in Boston with wife Mary from England on 13 May 1639/40.  They had two sons, John & Hope (Hosea) M. Couve with them.  They basically settled westernly R.I. and grandson, James married Sarah Lamphere.  His son married Mary Lamphere, his cousin.  Source; Provided by Carlton Frost, Prodigy, NHFN79A.
  --
Received the following information that is slightly different from Stan Piercefield, 200 Bailliere Dr., Martinsville, IN  46151-1305 on 9/7/1996: James Covey d: in Kings Co., Rhode Island
--
Another death date & place found in research: 1782 Washington Co., VA
